summary: Joel Spolsky warns software developers against signing NDAs and employment contracts containing non-compete clauses, no-hire clauses, and anti-poaching provisions. He argues these clauses restrict career mobility and primarily benefit employers who fail to retain employees through good treatment. Drawing on his experience at Microsoft, where a non-compete theoretically prevented him from working in software for a year, he shows how broadly written these restrictions can be. He encourages developers to leverage their market demand to negotiate these clauses out of contracts, and suggests that companies should focus on treating employees well rather than using legal restrictions to enforce loyalty.
key_points: Non-compete clauses in employment contracts can effectively prevent skilled workers from working in their field for 1-2 years, even though they are rarely enforced and legally unenforceable in some states like California

No-hire clauses that prevent you from hiring former colleagues after leaving are especially damaging because they cut off your best source of partners, employees, and professional references for future ventures
Developers in high demand have significant leverage to negotiate these clauses out of contracts, as demonstrated by Juno employees who successfully had a non-compete removed
If an employer insists on a non-compete, developers should counter by demanding continued salary payment for the restricted period
Companies should retain employees through good treatment rather than contractual restrictions on their future careers
money_quotes: If your employees are so eager to leave, that's your problem, not mine.
You need to keep your employees loyal by treating them well, not by creating arbitrary obstacles in their career.
Because Microsoft has a finger in everything related to software, technically I could not work in my field AT ALL for 12 months after leaving Microsoft.
If they are not going to enforce it, why is it in there?
You don't want me to work for a year after I leave, that's fine, but if I'm going to be 'on the beach', I want you to keep paying me my salary for one year after I leave.
I think that companies need to keep their employees loyal by treating them well, not by enforcing blind loyalty though a contract.
Your single best source of potential partners and employees are the people you know professionally. It is definitely not in your best interest to sign this clause.
key_insight: Restrictive employment clauses like non-competes and no-hire agreements are tools of companies that can't retain talent through good treatment, and skilled developers have enough market leverage to refuse them.
